request.question: "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, the best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with which Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards?

title: Everybody Loves a Lover
Dance with Me (Debelah Morgan song)
I'm Not at All in Love
Jerry Ross (composer)
Rags to Riches (song)
Steam Heat
Hernando's Hideaway
John Murray Anderson's Almanac
Whatever Lola Wants
Percy Faith
sentences: "Everybody Loves a Lover" is a popular song which was a hit single for Doris Day in 1958.
Its lyricist, Richard Adler, and its composer, Robert Allen, were both best known for collaborations with other partners.
The music Allen composed, aside from this song, was usually for collaborations with Al Stillman, and Adler wrote the lyrics after the 1955 death of his usual composing partner, Jerry Ross.
"Dance with Me" is a song by American R&B singer Debelah Morgan, composed by Morgan and music producer Giloh Morgan.
Released in the year 2000, it was the first single produced from Morgan's third studio album of the same name.
Its protagonist declares that she only wants to dance with her lover that night.
"Dance with Me" peaked at <nowiki>#</nowiki>8 on the "Billboard" Singles Chart on December 26, 2000 (after reaching the Top 40 on October 3), and has been added to many hit compilation albums.
"Dance with Me" received international acclaim and has been performed in different versions, including a special Walt Disney version and a Spanish version "Baila Conmigo."
Morgan's "Dance With Me" was based on "Hernando's Hideaway" written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, who are given credit on the song.
"I'm Not at All in Love" is a popular song written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, published in 1954.
It was first presented in the musical "The Pajama Game" by Janis Paige.

Jerry Ross (born Jerold Rosenberg; March 9, 1926 – November 11, 1955) was an American lyricist and composer whose works with Richard Adler for the musical theater include "The Pajama Game" and "Damn Yankees", winners of Tony Awards in 1955 and 1956, respectively, in both the "Best Musical" and "Best Composer and Lyricist" categories.
"Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.
The best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra, was number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953 and became a gold record.

In the same year, a version by David Whitfield reached number three in the British charts and one by Billy Ward and his Dominoes with Jackie Wilson singing lead made number two on Billboard's R&B chart.
Later recordings by Sunny & the Sunliners (#45 in 1963) and Elvis Presley (#33 in 1971) also made the Billboard charts.
"Steam Heat" is a show tune from the 1954 Broadway musical "The Pajama Game", written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.
"Hernando's Hideaway" is a tango show tune, largely in long metre, from the musical "The Pajama Game", written by Jerry Ross and Richard Adler and published in 1954.
It was sung in the stage and film versions of the musical by Carol Haney.
John Murray Anderson's Almanac is a musical revue, featuring the music of the songwriting team of Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, as well as other composers.
It was conceived by John Murray Anderson.
"Whatever Lola Wants" is a popular song, sometimes rendered as "Whatever Lola Wants, Lola Gets".
The music and words were written by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross for the 1955 musical play "Damn Yankees".
The song is sung by Lola, the Devil's assistant, a part originated by Gwen Verdon, who reprised the role in the film.
The saying was inspired by Lola Montez, an Irish-born "Spanish dancer" and mistress of King Ludwig I of Bavaria, who later became a San Francisco Gold Rush vamp.
Percy Faith (April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976) was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.
He is often credited with popularizing the "easy listening" or "mood music" format.
Faith became a staple of American popular music in the 1950s and continued well into the 1960s.

Though his professional orchestra-leading career began at the height of the swing era, Faith refined and rethought orchestration techniques, including use of large string sections, to soften and fill out the brass-dominated popular music of the 1940s.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross, the best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with which Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards?
hop1_docs: Rags to Riches (song) | "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.  The best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra, was number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953 and became a gold record.  In the same year, a version by David Whitfield reached number three in the British charts and one by Billy Ward and his Dominoes with Jackie Wilson singing lead made number two on Billboard's R&B chart.  Later recordings by Sunny & the Sunliners (#45 in 1963) and Elvis Presley (#33 in 1971) also made the Billboard charts.

Percy Faith | Percy Faith (April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976) was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.  He is often credited with popularizing the "easy listening" or "mood music" format.  Faith became a staple of American popular music in the 1950s and continued well into the 1960s.  Though his professional orchestra-leading career began at the height of the swing era, Faith refined and rethought orchestration techniques, including use of large string sections, to soften and fill out the brass-dominated popular music of the 1940s.

Everybody Loves a Lover | "Everybody Loves a Lover" is a popular song which was a hit single for Doris Day in 1958.  Its lyricist, Richard Adler, and its composer, Robert Allen, were both best known for collaborations with other partners.  The music Allen composed, aside from this song, was usually for collaborations with Al Stillman, and Adler wrote the lyrics after the 1955 death of his usual composing partner, Jerry Ross.

Sleigh Ride | "Sleigh Ride" is a popular light orchestral Christmas music standard composed by Leroy Anderson.  The composer had the original idea for the piece during a heat wave in July 1946 and finished the work in February 1948.  It was originally an instrumental piece; the lyrics, about someone asking another to join them for a ride in a sleigh, were written by Mitchell Parish in 1950.  The orchestral version was first recorded in 1949 by Arthur Fiedler and the Boston Pops Orchestra.  Sleigh Ride was a hit record on RCA Victor Red Seal 49-0515 (45 rpm) / 10-1484 (78 rpm), and has become a signature song for the orchestra.  The 45 rpm version was originally issued on red vinyl.  The Pops has also recorded the song with John Williams, their conductor from 1979 to 1995, and Keith Lockhart, their current conductor.

Robert Russell Bennett | Robert Russell Bennett (June 15, 1894 – August 18, 1981) was an American composer and arranger, best known for his orchestration of many well-known Broadway and Hollywood musicals by other composers such as Irving Berlin, George Gershwin, Jerome Kern, Cole Porter, and Richard Rodgers.  In 1957 and 2008, Bennett received Tony Awards recognizing his orchestrations for Broadway shows.  Early in his career he was often billed as Russell Bennett.

Norrie Paramor | Norman William "Norrie" Paramor (15 May 1914 – 9 September 1979) was a British record producer, composer, arranger, pianist, bandleader, and orchestral conductor.  He is best known for his work with Cliff Richard and the Shadows, both together and separately, steering their early careers and producing and arranging most of their material from the late 1950s to the early 1970s.  Paramor was a composer of studio albums, theatrical productions, and film scores.

My Heart Tells Me | "My Heart Tells Me" is a song written by Harry Warren with lyrics by Mack Gordon.  It is the theme to the 1943 American musical film "Sweet Rosie O'Grady", in which it is sung by lead actress Betty Grable.  A 1940s standard, the song has been recorded by numerous artists, including Frank Sinatra, Tony Bennett, Nat King Cole, and Etta Jones.  The film's popularity contributed to the commercial success of the version of the song recorded by bandleader Glen Gray and his Casa Loma Orchestra with vocals by singer Eugenie Baird; titled "My Heart Tells Me (Should I Believe My Heart?)" , it topped "The Billboard"<nowiki>'s</nowiki> National Best Selling Retail Records chart for five weeks in 1944.
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "Rags to Riches" — passage [1] ("Rags to Riches (song)"): "1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross." [1]
  - Tony Bennett credit — passage [1]: "recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra." [1]
  - Percy Faith identity — passage [2] ("Percy Faith"): "Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor" and "known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards." [2]
- Ambiguities:
  - Other bandleader/arranger pages appear in the passages (possible distractors): "Robert Russell Bennett" [5] and "Norrie Paramor" [6]; neither is labeled Canadian in these passages.
  - No multiple Percy Faith variants are present in the provided passages.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity: Percy Faith (exactly as shown in the passages).
  - Missing fact needed to finish: the precise credited collaborator/billing on Tony Bennett's 1953 recording of "Rags to Riches" (exact artist credit as shown on the single/record release or reliable discography).
- Retrieval direction (priority order):
  1) Open "Rags to Riches (song)" page — check the lede/infobox and any "Recorded versions"/"Chart performance" or "Release" sections to confirm the wording of the Tony Bennett credit. (Search: "Rags to Riches Tony Bennett Percy Faith 1953 credit")
  2) Open "Percy Faith" page — check the lede/infobox and Career/Discography sections to verify his Canadian nationality and role as bandleader/orchestrator and any collaborations with Tony Bennett. (Search: "Percy Faith lede infobox Career 'Tony Bennett'")
  3) Open "Tony Bennett" page — check Discography or Singles section and any single-specific pages for the 1953 "Rags to Riches" release to verify the exact artist billing on the record. (Search: "Tony Bennett Rags to Riches single 1953 Percy Faith credit")
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved and the exact billing is confirmed.
response.hop2_query: Rags to Riches Tony Bennett Percy Faith 1953 credit
hop2_docs: Rags to Riches (song) | "Rags to Riches" is a 1953 popular song by Richard Adler and Jerry Ross.  The best-known version of the song, recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra, was number one for eight weeks on the Billboard chart in 1953 and became a gold record.  In the same year, a version by David Whitfield reached number three in the British charts and one by Billy Ward and his Dominoes with Jackie Wilson singing lead made number two on Billboard's R&B chart.  Later recordings by Sunny & the Sunliners (#45 in 1963) and Elvis Presley (#33 in 1971) also made the Billboard charts.

Percy Faith | Percy Faith (April 7, 1908 – February 9, 1976) was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor, known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.  He is often credited with popularizing the "easy listening" or "mood music" format.  Faith became a staple of American popular music in the 1950s and continued well into the 1960s.  Though his professional orchestra-leading career began at the height of the swing era, Faith refined and rethought orchestration techniques, including use of large string sections, to soften and fill out the brass-dominated popular music of the 1940s.

Al Caiola | Alexander Emil Caiola (September 7, 1920 – November 9, 2016) was a guitarist, composer and arranger who spanned a variety of music genres including jazz, country, rock, and pop.  He recorded over fifty albums and worked with some of the biggest names in music during the 20th century, including Elvis Presley, Ferrante & Teicher, Frank Sinatra, Percy Faith, Buddy Holly, Mitch Miller, and Tony Bennett.  During World War II Caiola played with the United States Marine Corps 5th Marine Division Band that also included Bob Crosby.  Caiola served in the Battle of Iwo Jima as a stretcher bearer.
Rags to riches (disambiguation) | Rags to riches refers to a rise from poverty to wealth.
Andrew Percy Bennett | Andrew Percy Bennett (30 July 1866 – 3 November 1943) was a British diplomat.
Tony Bennett: The Playground | Tony Bennett: The Playground is an album by Tony Bennett, released in 1998.

Tony Bennett's "Something" | Tony Bennett's "Something" is a 1970 studio album by Tony Bennett.  As with Bennett's previous album, "Tony Sings the Great Hits of Today! ", it contains renditions of contemporary pop songs, including "Something", which had appeared on the earlier release.
response.summary_2: recorded by Tony Bennett with Percy Faith and his orchestra. Percy Faith was a Canadian bandleader, orchestrator, composer and conductor known for his lush arrangements of pop and Christmas standards.
